# Soil Microbiome Diversity and Drought Resilience in Temperate Grasslands

## Abstract

We tested whether microbial diversity buffers grassland productivity against drought. Across 18 plots
over two seasons, high-diversity plots retained 31% more aboveground biomass under imposed drought (95% CI 18 to 44)[^plots-2025].
The effect is consistent with prior work linking rhizosphere diversity to water-use
efficiency[^prior-rev]. We conclude that diversity is associated with — not necessarily causal of —
drought resilience at this scale.

## Introduction

Drought frequency in temperate grasslands has risen over recent decades[^climate-2024], and the
ecosystem services these systems provide are sensitive to soil biology. Prior reviews establish that
microbial communities influence plant water uptake[^prior-rev], but field-scale evidence under
controlled drought remains limited. This study addresses that gap by manipulating diversity directly
and measuring biomass response under a rainfall-exclusion treatment.

## Methods

We established 18 plots of one square metre each in a temperate grassland (mean annual precipitation
780 mm). Microbial diversity was manipulated by inoculating sterilized soil with dilution gradients
of a common inoculum, yielding low-, medium-, and high-diversity treatments (n = 6 each), verified by
16S rRNA amplicon sequencing[^plots-2025]. Drought was imposed with rainout shelters excluding 60% of ambient rainfall (source: protocol in plots-2025)
over a 10-week summer period. Aboveground biomass was harvested, dried at 60 degrees Celsius, and
weighed. Treatment effects were estimated with a linear mixed model (diversity as fixed effect, block
as random effect); effect sizes are reported as 95% confidence intervals from 2,000 bootstrap resamples (source: analysis plan in plots-2025).

## Results

High-diversity plots retained 31% more biomass than low-diversity plots under drought (95% CI 18 to 44, p = 0.003)[^plots-2025].
Medium-diversity plots were intermediate, retaining 16% more biomass (95% CI 4 to 28)[^plots-2025].
The diversity-biomass relationship was monotonic across the gradient. No significant difference
appeared in the well-watered controls, indicating the effect was drought-specific.

## Discussion

The results support an association between microbial diversity and drought resilience, consistent
with the rhizosphere mechanisms reviewed previously[^prior-rev]. Because diversity was manipulated
but soil chemistry covaried with the dilution gradient, we frame the finding as associative rather
than strictly causal. Limitations include the single-site design and one drought intensity. Future
work should replicate across sites and manipulate water-use pathways directly. The contribution is
the first field-scale, manipulated-diversity test under controlled drought in this system.

## References

[^plots-2025]: Author et al. (2025). Field manipulation dataset and 16S sequencing, grassland drought experiment. Repository DOI 10.0000/plots2025.
[^prior-rev]: Reviewer, B. (2023). Rhizosphere diversity and plant water-use efficiency: a review. Journal of Soil Ecology, 41(2), 110–129.
[^climate-2024]: Climate Panel (2024). Regional drought frequency trends in temperate biomes. Climate Reports, 18, 1–22.
